title: Giving Medications Through a Feeding Tube
subtitle: The nurse gives meds through an NG/PEG tube — verifying placement, using liquids when possible, never crushing extended-release/enteric-coated pills, giving each med separately, and flushing with water.

patient.name: Enteral Tube Medications
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely administers medications through an enteral feeding tube.

vitals.temp: Give each med one at a time

labs.v: Crush IR tablets to fine powder.
Give one med at a time.
Do not add meds to feeding bag.

nursesNote: The nurse safely administers medications through an enteral feeding tube (NG or PEG). Tube placement is verified before anything is instilled, and the head of the bed is kept elevated 30-45 degrees to reduce aspiration risk. Liquid forms are used whenever available because they clog the tube less; when only tablets exist, the nurse crushes immediate-release tablets to a fine powder and dissolves them in water, but NEVER crushes extended-release (SR/XL/CR), enteric-coated, or sublingual products, since crushing destroys the delivery mechanism and can cause a dangerous dose dump or gastric irritation. Each medication is given separately, one at a time, with a water flush before, between, and after the medications (commonly about 15-30 mL) to keep the tube patent and prevent drug interactions. Medications are not added to the feeding bag. Certain drugs (such as phenytoin) require the tube feeding to be held before and after the dose to ensure absorption. If the tube clogs, warm water and gentle pressure are used; the tube is never forced. The standard rights and three checks still apply.
providerOrders: Verify feeding-tube placement before giving any medication
Keep the head of the bed elevated 30-45 degrees
Use liquid forms; never crush extended-release or enteric-coated pills
Give each medication separately and flush with water before, between, and after
Do not add medications to the feeding bag

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about giving meds via feeding tube?

opts.text: Tube placement is verified before giving meds
Liquid forms are preferred when available
Extended-release tablets can be crushed for the tube
Each medication is given separately
The tube is flushed with water before and after
The head of the bed is kept elevated

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What guides safe enteral med delivery?

opts.text: Crushing an enteric-coated pill can cause gastric irritation
Crushing extended-release can cause a dangerous dose dump
Medications should be mixed into the feeding bag
Some drugs need the tube feeding held around the dose
Flushing between meds prevents drug interactions and clogs
A clogged tube should be forced with high pressure

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order giving a tablet through a PEG tube

opts.text: Verify tube placement and raise the head of the bed
Flush with water to confirm patency
Dissolve the crushed immediate-release med in water
Instill the med, then flush again
Keep HOB up and document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Safe to crush or NOT safe to crush?

opts.text: A plain immediate-release tablet
An extended-release (XL) tablet
An enteric-coated aspirin
A liquid suspension
A sublingual tablet
A scored, uncoated tablet ordered crushed

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The tube resists when you try to instill the med
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop and do not force the medication
Check for kinks and verify placement
Try a gentle warm-water flush
Notify the provider if it stays clogged
Document the problem and actions
Verifying placement before instilling medication
Crushing an extended-release tablet for the tube
Flushing with water before, between, and after meds
Adding all the meds to the feeding bag at once
Giving each medication separately
Keeping the head of the bed flat during dosing
Holding the feeding around a phenytoin dose
Forcing a clogged tube with high pressure

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
